sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To configure customer key management, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)
access to the project.

Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves to the
project as a `Project Owner`.

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys with AWS KMS
sections.body:
Customer key management in Atlas follows a process called envelope encryption.
This process creates multiple layers of encryption by encrypting one key with another
key. To enable customer key management, Atlas uses the following encryption keys:

sections.heading: Example
sections.body:
Consider the following encryption hierarchy for a three-node replica set.
Atlas uses the CMK (customer-managed key) from Google Cloud KMS to encrypt a unique
MongoDB Master Key for each node in the cluster. Each node also contains
three databases, each of which is encrypted with a unique
per-database encryption key. When the cluster starts up,
Atlas decrypts the MongoDB Master Key by using the CMK (customer-managed key) from
Google Cloud KMS and supplies this to the MongoDB Server.

> **Note:** If you revoke Atlas's access to the CMK (customer-managed key), Atlas shuts down the
> nodes in your cluster and you can't access your data until
> you restore access to the CMK (customer-managed key).

![Diagram of CMK workflow with AWS KMS and Atlas](/images/aws-kms-cmk-diagram.png)

sections.heading: Rotate your AWS Customer Master Key
sections.body:
> **Note:** This feature is not available for any of the following deployments:
>
> - Free clusters
>
> - Flex clusters
>
> To learn more, see [Limits](limits).

sections.heading: MongoDB Master Key - MongoDB Responsibility
sections.body:
When you use your own cloud provider KMS (Key Management Service), Atlas automatically rotates
MongoDB Master Keys at least every 90 days. Your key rotation will begin
during a [Configure Maintenance Window](configure-maintenance-window),
if you have one configured. Deferring maintenance (either manually or automatically)
may cause the key to be rotated past the 90-day mark.
Keys are rotated on a
rolling basis and the process does not require the data to be rewritten.

sections.heading: Your AWS (Amazon Web Services) CMK (customer-managed key) - Your Responsibility
sections.body:
Atlas does *not* automatically rotate the AWS (Amazon Web Services) CMK (customer-managed key) used for
AWS (Amazon Web Services)-provided Encryption at Rest.

As a best practice, Atlas creates an [alert](<AWS encryption key elapsed time since last rotation is above (n) days>) to remind you
to rotate your AWS (Amazon Web Services) CMK (customer-managed key) every 90 days by default when you
[enable Encryption at Rest](aws-enable-cluster-encryption-at-rest) for an Atlas
project. You can configure the time period of this alert.

You can rotate your AWS (Amazon Web Services) CMK (customer-managed key) yourself or configure your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service)
instance to [automatically rotate](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/rotate-keys.html) your CMK (customer-managed key). If
you configure automatic AWS (Amazon Web Services) CMK (customer-managed key) rotation, the default time period
for rotation is approximately 365 days.

If you have already set up an automatic CMK (customer-managed key) rotation in AWS (Amazon Web Services) and
don't want to receive the Atlas alert to rotate your CMK (customer-managed key) every 90
days, you can [Configure Alert Settings](configure-alerts) the default alert period
to be greater than 365 days or [disable](disable-alert-setting)
the alert.

This page explains how to create a new key and update the CMK (customer-managed key) ID in
Atlas to rotate your Atlas project CMK (customer-managed key). This method of key
rotation supports more granular control of the rotation period compared
to AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) automatic CMK (customer-managed key) rotation.

> **Important:** 
>
> Cloud Backups with Encryption at Rest
>
> For clusters using Encryption at Rest and
> [Cloud Backup](backup-cloud-provider), Atlas uses the project's CMK (customer-managed key) and
> AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM (Identity and Access Management) user credentials at the time of the snapshot to
> automatically encrypt the snapshot data files. This is an additional
> layer of encryption on the existing encryption applied to all
> Atlas storage and snapshot volumes.
>
> Atlas does *not* re-encrypt snapshots with the new CMK (customer-managed key) after
> rotation. Do *not* delete the old CMK (customer-managed key) until you check *every*
> backup-enabled cluster in the project for any snapshots still using
> that CMK (customer-managed key). Atlas deletes backups in accordance to the
> [base snapshot schedule](cloud-provider-retention-policy). After Atlas deletes all
> snapshots depending on a given CMK (customer-managed key), you can delete that CMK (customer-managed key)
> safely.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
- Enter the following information:

  | Field                      | Action                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
  | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | AWS IAM role               | Select an existing AWS (Amazon Web Services) IAM role that already has access to your KMS (Key Management Service) keys, or authorize a new role and grant this role access to your KMS (Key Management Service) keys with the following permissions:DescribeKeyEncryptDecryptTo learn more, see Role-Based Access to Your Encryption Key for a Project. |
  | Customer Master Key ID     | Enter your AWS customer master key ID.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
  | Customer Master Key Region | Select the AWS (Amazon Web Services) region in which you created your AWS (Amazon Web Services) CMK (customer-managed key).Atlas lists only AWS (Amazon Web Services) regions that support AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service).                                                                                                       |

- Click Save.

Atlas displays a banner in the Atlas console during the
CMK (customer-managed key) rotation process. Do **not** delete or disable the CMK (customer-managed key) until
your changes have deployed.

sections.heading: Reconfigure AWS KMS Region During an Outage
sections.body:
During a regional outage, your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) region might
become unavailable. If you've enabled [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption),
you can perform encrypt and decrypt operations while at least one node
is still available. However, if all nodes become unavailable,
you can't perform cryptographic operations. A node becomes unavailable
if it restarts during the outage.

To get the unavailable nodes to a healthy state, you
can reconfigure your current AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) region to an available region.
To change your KMS (Key Management Service) region, your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) key must be a [multi-Region key](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview).
To create a multi-Region Key, see the [AWS documentation](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-create).

> **Note:** You can't convert a single-Region key to a multi-Region Key.

sections.heading: Procedure
sections.body:
To reconfigure your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) region, complete the following steps
in Atlas:

To ensure that Atlas doesn't re-encrypt your data, verify that the
AWS IAM role and Customer Master Key ID reflect
your existing credentials.

Select another AWS (Amazon Web Services) region for which you have configured
your multi-Region key.

sections.heading: Next Steps
sections.body:
You can use a customer-managed key (CMK) from your AWS (Amazon Web Services) KMS (Key Management Service) over
a public network or over AWS PrivateLink. To learn more, see
the following:

- [Manage Customer Keys with AWS Over a Public Network](security-aws-kms-public-network)

- [Manage Customer Keys with AWS Over Private Endpoints](security-aws-kms-pvt-endpoint)

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for Search Nodes
sections.body:
By default, MongoDB and [search processes](about-mongot) run on the same nodes.
With this architecture, customer-managed encryption applies to your database data,
but it does not apply to search indexes.

When you enable [Search Nodes](what-is-search-node), search processes run on separate nodes. This allows
you to enable [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption),
so you can encrypt both database data and search indexes with the same
customer-managed keys for comprehensive encryption coverage.

> **Note:** Database nodes and Search Nodes use different encryption methods
> with the same customer-managed keys. Database nodes use the
> [WiredTiger Encrypted Storage Engine](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest),
> while Search Nodes use encryption at the disk level.

sections.heading: Related Topics
sections.body:
- To learn more about MongoDB Encryption at Rest, see
  [Encryption at Rest](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest) in
  the MongoDB server documentation.

- To learn more about Encryption at Rest with Cloud Backups, see
  [Storage Engine and Cloud Backup Encryption](encrypted-cloud-provider-snapshot).
